images.caption: Figure 1:High-quality samples generated by UniCom. Built on compressed continuous representations, our unified multimodal model demonstrates exceptional capabilities in text-to-image generation, precise image editing, and fine-grained controllable generation.

Figure 2:Overview of the proposed framework.For a controlled comparison, both pathways are built upon the same compressed representations and jointly optimized with cross-entropy loss (ℒc​e\mathcal{L}_{ce}) and flow matching loss (ℒf​m\mathcal{L}_{fm}).

Figure 3:Comparison of the results for image editing, highlighting UniCom performance in tasks such as image manipulation, object swapping, and color adjustment. See more visualization in the Appendix.

Figure 4:Overview of the proposed diffusion decoder and reconstruction analysis.Given an input image, the vision encoder extracts semantic features which are then processed by compressor and decompressor to condition the diffusion model for image reconstruction.
During training, the compression modules and the diffusion backbone are optimized, while the encoder remains frozen.
We investigate the bottleneck capacity by varying the token numbernnand feature dimensiondd.
As illustrated in the bottom row, compressing the dimensionddyields superior reconstruction fidelity, whereas reducing the token numbernnleads to noticeable blurring in fine details.

Figure 5:Visual comparison of image reconstruction results.
Notably, our method (d64) maintains high fidelity in high-frequency details (e.g., text characters) and preserves facial identity better than these semantic-based baselines, achieving quality comparable to the specialized Flux.1-dev VAE.
Figure 6:Feature dimension compression accelerates T2I training.Compressing tod=64d=64achieves3.8×3.8\timesfaster convergence than original SigLIP features.
Figure 6:Feature dimension compression accelerates T2I training.Compressing tod=64d=64achieves3.8×3.8\timesfaster convergence than original SigLIP features.
Figure 7:VLM initialization and Transfusion architecture improve generation.We report GenEval results during unify pre-training.

Figure 8:T-SNE visualization of feature distributions.We randomly select 6 distinct classes from ImageNet, sampling 150 images per class.
MHA compression to 64-d preserves the structure, MLP projection yields scattered 64-d features, and FLUX-VAE has no semantic separability due to pixel-reconstruction focus.

header: Appendix CImpact of Dimensionality Compression on Training

images.caption: Figure 9:Reducing dimensions maintains higher reconstruction fidelity than sequence reduction.We compare the reconstruction training dynamics (Loss and PSNR) of different compressed representations (𝐳~\tilde{\mathbf{z}}).
Figure 10:Additional visual comparison of image reconstruction results.

Figure 11:Qualitative reconstruction samples using Decoder (d64).Left:Reconstructed image.Right:Original image.
Even with an18×18\timeschannel compression ratio, our model accurately recovers complex high-frequency details.
Figure 12:Our unified prediction architecture (UniCom) preserves finer spatial layout and structural consistency, while query-based methods struggle with spatial correspondence and editing fidelity.
Figure 12:Our unified prediction architecture (UniCom) preserves finer spatial layout and structural consistency, while query-based methods struggle with spatial correspondence and editing fidelity.

header: Appendix EMore Results of Image Editing

images.caption: Figure 13:More results on single image editing.From top to bottom: (1)Add/Remove/Extract: adding objects, removing elements, and extracting subjects; (2)Replace: substituting objects or materials while preserving context; (3)Style Transfer: transforming image appearance into different artistic styles; (4)Background: modifying or replacing image backgrounds; (5)Subject-Driven Generation: generating new images conditioned on reference subjects; (6)Controllable Generation: generating images with spatial or structural control signals.
Figure 14:More results on multi-element composition.Our model can compose multiple reference subjects into a coherent scene while preserving the identity of each element.

Figure 15:More results on intelligent image editing.These examples require complex world knowledge such as biological transformations, geographical understanding, and cultural context to perform accurate editing.

Figure 16:Chain-of-thought enhanced generation and editing.For each example, we show the generated image along with the corresponding thinking process enclosed in<think>tokens. The model first reasons about the required visual transformation, then generates the output image accordingly.
